danger_signals.visual_form: Sprite-based hostile creatures with distinct visual designs
danger_signals.meaning: Direct combat threat requiring evasion or weapon use

danger_signals.visual_form: Spike obstacles in level geometry (more prevalent in US/EU versions)
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental danger requiring precise platforming navigation

danger_signals.visual_form: Vertical drops in level layout (more numerous in US/EU versions)
danger_signals.meaning: Death hazard requiring careful movement and jumping

danger_signals.visual_form: Specific enemy type with expanded hitbox (larger in Japanese version)
danger_signals.meaning: High-difficulty threat with generous collision detection

safety_signals.visual_form: Level-end states that replenish health (Japanese version mechanic)
safety_signals.meaning: Safe progression milestone, restoration of player resources

safety_signals.visual_form: Environmental items (super tomato in laboratory, matchstick bow in later sections)
safety_signals.meaning: Empowerment zones and resource availability for combat readiness

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Gizmo can be damaged by enemy Gremlins and Mohawk boss character
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Laboratory setting with tomato weapons suggests allied scientific base

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Distinct sprite designs for various gremlin types
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple gremlin enemy types including frag mines and Mohawk (final boss)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Presence throughout building levels, culminating in Gremlin Control Center

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Distinctive punk-styled gremlin with mohawk haircut (movie character)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Can attack Gizmo's starting position (Japanese/prototype versions)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Boss encounter territory, strategic threat positioning

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Controlled indoor environment with technological elements; super tomato weapon available
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Gizmo equipped with tomato-based combat tool; contained gremlin threat

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Shift to bow weapons using matchsticks; increased environmental hazards
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Combat style changes reflecting available arsenals; escalating difficulty and enemy variety

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Gremlin Control Center as final destination
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Convergence of all threats; final confrontation with gremlin infestation

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Fewer spikes and pits in Stage 2-2; larger frag mine hitboxes
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Adjusted challenge curve; modified enemy collision behavior; health restoration between levels

environmental_storytelling.example: Laboratory environment introduces super tomato weapon; later sections feature matchstick bow—visual communication of player advancement through building locations
Regional differences in spike and pit concentration communicate relative danger zones and game difficulty philosophy across versions
Mohawk's position at Gizmo's starting location (in Japanese/prototype versions) telegraphs immediate threat and aggressive enemy AI behavior
Multi-stage building layout from laboratory to control center creates spatial narrative of gremlin infestation escalation and player mission progression

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Platforming movement with equipped weapon; vulnerable to gremlin contact
Can attack enemies with context-specific weapons (tomato or bow)
Health restoration occurs after level completion; altered survival economy
Sprite-based movement patterns; collision-based threat detection
Expanded hitbox makes evasion more challenging (Japanese version); precise timing required
Can strike Gizmo's starting position in Japanese/prototype; demonstrates active threat intent rather than reactive gameplay

notes: Gremlins 2 NES communicates world-state primarily through spatial progression (laboratory→building→control center) and weapon availability shifts. Danger is signaled through enemy sprite design, environmental hazard density, and boss positioning. Faction identity is minimal—communication relies on protagonist-vs-antagonist binary (Gizmo vs. Gremlins). Regional variants indicate deliberate tuning of visual difficulty communication: Japanese version reduces visual hazards but increases enemy collision aggression. Movie tie-in nature suggests sprite designs carry pre-established faction identity from film source material rather than deriving purely from game systems.
